name: Regenerant Catalunya
leads: Luiz Fernando Gomez Segala, with Giulio Quarta & Andrea Farias
place: Rural Catalonia and the streets of L'Hospitalet, Spain
tagline: Bridging Catalan cooperatives and Web3 — money still to follow.
whyItMattered: Catalonia has one of Europe's most developed cooperative economies, but deep skepticism toward crypto. This was an honest attempt to test whether on-chain tools could strengthen that work without betraying its values — and a candid record of where the rails reach grassroots communities and where they don't.
story: Regenerant Catalunya set out to channel matching funds into regenerative projects through two established cooperative networks: Miceli in rural Catalonia, and La Fundició in the dense, working-class streets of L'Hospitalet. The team raised €11,000 of local co-funding and put it on-chain before the Localism Fund grant even arrived.

The grant landed late, on 16 December 2025, at roughly half the figure first floated. That delay cascaded into the holiday break, compressed Phase 1, and triggered a hard trust test — the kickoff ran before the matching money was in. Eleven projects were onboarded; eight new wallets opened; 29 Karma activity reports filed across both networks.

Then the team pivoted honestly. Rather than force one approach, they tailored per network: Miceli, more receptive, onto a Safe multisig to co-manage funds; La Fundició, opting out of crypto entirely, toward a compliant bank off-ramp and fiat conviction voting. Off-ramping in Spain, they found, was close to inviable without a legal entity.

Today the books reconcile on-chain to the dollar — the cleanest in the cohort. But the defining fact is the one the report doesn't dress up: nearly six months after payout, $0 has reached any local project. The full $18,000 matching plus $6,622 of co-funding still sits in the Safe. The only money that moved was the operator's coordination fee.

signatureStory.title: "How do we know this isn't a scam?"
signatureStory.text: At the kickoff workshop, only the local co-funding had been on-ramped — the Localism Fund money hadn't arrived, and the matching figure had already shrunk. A participant put the unease into words directly, in front of the room: how do we know you won't run away with our money? The team had braced for crypto skepticism from Catalan cooperative circles, but the moment crystallised why: in a movement built on trust, any inconsistency — late funds, a changing number — reads as a red flag. The lesson they drew was blunt: working with these communities means being overzealous with transparency, staying ahead of the question before it's asked.
signatureStory.quote: How do we know this isn't a scam? How do we know you're not going to run away with our money?
signatureStory.quoteAttribution: A kickoff workshop participant, recounted in the grantee report, 5.3

delivered: Secured and on-ramped €11,000 (~$12,432) of local co-funding from Miceli and La Fundició before the grant arrived — verified on-chain to the dollar
Onboarded 11 projects across two cooperative networks (rural Miceli + urban La Fundició), with 8 new wallets and 29 Karma activity reports filed
Ran 5 workshops (kickoff, Karma onboarding, wallet setup, Safe multisig) and built a trilingual EN/ES/CA public knowledge base plus an open GitHub methodology repo
Pivoted mid-stream from project-level to network-level engagement, tailoring tools to each partner's actual readiness rather than imposing a uniform approach
Established a Safe multisig on Celo and began onboarding Miceli project leads as co-signers for collective fund governance
Did not distribute any grant funds — $24,622 remains held in the Safe; the only outflow is the $5,812 operations fee

outcomes.text: Miceli invited ReFi Barcelona to become a member cooperative and a collaborator on its Bioregional Financing Facility — a structural partnership well beyond the program's scope

Funds committed to 11 regenerative projects across two networks, but distribution still pending — the first concrete channel between global ReFi and the Catalan solidarity economy is open, not yet flowing
A documented methodology for bridging cooperative/ESS ecosystems with Web3 — including, honestly, where the tooling still fails grassroots communities
Collective fund governance piloted per network: a Safe multisig for Miceli, fiat conviction voting for La Fundició — both still onboarding

status: Wrapping up operationally; funds committed but $0 distributed, off-ramp unresolved

verdict.line: A candid, financially transparent, well-anchored program whose books reconcile on-chain to the dollar, held down by the fact that its core deliverable has not been met: $0 of the matching has reached any local project, and the full $18,000 still sits in the Safe nearly six months after payout.

honesty: This is the cohort's cleanest financial report — every figure, including the uncomfortable ones, reconciles on-chain to the dollar, and the co-funding genuinely arrived early. But the central fact is a delivery one, not an integrity one: nearly six months after payout no grant money has reached a single project, and running the entire program without distributing funds is reported as a timing detail rather than named as the material gap it is.

proposed.summary: Channel matching funds into Catalan bioregional projects through two established cooperative networks.
points: Curate a cohort of 10–12 regenerative projects via partner networks, no open call
Disburse ≥€1,000 per project in two 50/50 phases across Nov 2025–Feb 2026
Collect on-chain Karma GAP impact records and run a human-council evaluation
Pilot Web3/ReFi tools and publish a replicable trilingual cooperative-round playbook

stories.title: Miceli opens the door
stories.text: The program's deepest result was relational. Miceli — a second-degree cooperative serving rural Catalonia — invited ReFi Barcelona to become a member cooperative and a collaborator on its Bioregional Financing Facility. Cooperatives don't extend membership lightly; this positioned the hub inside Miceli's governance, not outside as a service provider.

stories.title: Two networks, two strategies
stories.text: Rather than impose one approach on eleven projects, the team split Phase 2 by readiness. Miceli, more receptive, onboarded onto a Safe multisig to co-manage funds on-chain. La Fundició, opting out of crypto entirely, was routed toward a compliant bank off-ramp and fiat conviction voting through Hum Community — same participatory logic, different entry point.

stories.title: Onboarding before the money
stories.text: Across two cooperative networks, eleven projects — from Fluvià watershed regeneration to a self-managed community kitchen in La Florida — were onboarded in Phase 1. Eight new wallets opened and 29 Karma activity reports documented 2025 work and 2026 plans, all before the matching grant had even arrived on-chain.

dimensionEvidence.evidence: The strongest dimension: most of the ReFi Barcelona team was physically present in communities throughout — living near La Fundició, visiting partners, meeting people in their homes and workspaces. La Fundició specifically serves working-class, migrant, and Romani communities in L'Hospitalet, populations typically underserved by funding programs.

Most participating projects are cooperatives with real economic operations — selling services, producing goods, employing people — embedded in established local networks, not grant-dependent NGOs. The program channeled funds into existing economic circuits; €11,000 in local co-funding committed before global funds arrived showed these networks already invest in each other.

Ecological impact flows through the grantees' own work rather than activities the program ran: Simbiosi Fluvial regenerating the Fluvià river watershed, Resilience Earth advising municipalities on bioregional governance, and La Granja del Tilo preserving agroecological systems in the Parc Agrari del Baix Llobregat. The program kept an ecological lens via bioregionalism but ran no restoration directly.

correlation.proposed: Disburse ≥€1,000 per project in two 50/50 phases across Nov 2025–Feb 2026
correlation.delivered: $0 disbursed to local projects; the full $24,622 (18,000 cUSD + 6,622 USDC) remains held intact in the program Safe nearly six months after payout

correlation.proposed: Run a human-council evaluation (adapted from GG23) over Phase 1 allocations
correlation.delivered: Dropped in favour of a flat €1,000-per-project allocation — a sensible simplification for a small curated cohort

correlation.proposed: Collect on-chain Karma GAP impact records across the cohort
correlation.delivered: Delivered: 11 projects filed 29 Karma activity reports — though decoupled from any funding consequence, and in a format projects are unlikely to reuse

correlation.proposed: Pilot Web3/ReFi adoption tools (Silvi, Gainforest, Hypercerts/Ecocerts, Sarafu, dMRV)
correlation.delivered: Essentially none delivered; one real adoption — Miceli's Safe multisig — with signer onboarding still in progress

correlation.proposed: Use streamed releases (Octant V2 / Flowstate / Gardens) for Phase 2
correlation.delivered: Not used; not addressed in the report

correlation.proposed: Secure ≥€11,000 (~$12,100) local co-funding from the two partner networks
correlation.delivered: Delivered and verified on-chain to the dollar: $12,432 arrived before the grant, from Miceli (€6,000) and La Fundició (€5,000)

timeline.what: Co-funding raised on-chain from Miceli and La Fundició
Localism Fund $18,000 matching grant received on Celo
$5,812 operations fee paid; grant funds still held
Miceli invited ReFi Barcelona as member cooperative

operatorChecks: Confirm $0 has reached any of the 11 projects ~6 months post-payout — the full $18,000 grant still sits in the Celo Safe.
Examine the conflict of interest: the only outflow ($5,812 ops fee) went to operator-associated wallets, one named in the application.
Correct the 2.1 status box — it claims 'some funds distributed' but no local-project funds have moved on-chain.
Confirm whether Miceli (€6,000) and La Fundició (€5,000) are two distinct co-funders — both arrived from one EOA.
Verify whether the Phase-2 Web3 pilots (Silvi, Gainforest, Hypercerts, Sarafu) ran — the report itemises almost none.
Confirm a distribution plan and date for the $24,622 held undistributed, or whether the round is closing without disbursing.
fundingNote: Recorded disbursements are empty because, nearly six months after payout, the full $18,000 grant (plus ~$6,622 of co-funding) still sits untouched in the program Safe. ReFi Barcelona ran the whole wrapper — onboarding 11 projects, workshops, a trilingual knowledge base — but never executed the two-stage 50/50 distribution; the only money that moved was a $5,812 operations fee to operator-associated wallets.
